threats.title: Public Access to Cred Tracking Arrays Allows Permanent Freezing of User Shares
threats.hypothesis_description: The internal tracking functions `_addCredIdPerAddress` and `_removeCredIdPerAddress` are declared as public and lack access control. An attacker can call `_removeCredIdPerAddress` for any victim's address, corrupting their internal index mapping. When the victim later attempts to sell their shares, the internal `_updateCuratorShareBalance` function will attempt to remove the credId again, look up the corrupted index, and revert with `WrongCredId`. This permanently freezes the victim's shares and underlying ETH value.
threats.investigation_rationale: Public state-mutating functions that manage internal accounting arrays are a severe vulnerability. Validation is needed to confirm that the index corruption reliably causes a revert during the sell flow.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Public visibility on internal array management functions without access control

preconditions: Victim owns at least one share of a cred

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Index lookup on corrupted mapping: `uint256 indexToRemove = _credIdsPerAddressCredIdIndex[sender_][credId_];`
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker deletes the index mapping externally, causing internal sell logic to revert
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit for attacker, but causes complete denial of service and permanent loss of funds for the victim (griefing/ransom potential)

economic_questions: Does the corrupted index reliably cause a revert in all sell scenarios?
Can the victim recover by buying another share to reset the index?
validation_requirements: Write a PoC where attacker calls `_removeCredIdPerAddress` on a victim, then victim calls `sellShareCred`

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Destroys user trust and permanently locks liquidity in the bonding curve

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% loss of invested ETH in the specific Cred
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire TVL of the Cred bonding curve is at risk of being frozen

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Array swap-and-pop logic behavior when index is 0
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Check if `credId_ != credIdToRemove` reliably catches the corruption

threats.title: Attacker Can Drain Trapped Factory ETH to Mint NFTs for Free
threats.hypothesis_description: The `claim` function in `PhiFactory` forwards the exact `mintFee` to an internal `this.merkleClaim{value: mintFee}` call. If a user overpays, the excess ETH is trapped in the factory because the inner refund logic only sees the exact `mintFee`. An attacker can exploit this trapped ETH by calling `claim` with `msg.value = 0`. The internal `this.merkleClaim{value: mintFee}` call will succeed by using the factory's trapped ETH balance, allowing the attacker to mint NFTs and generate protocol/creator rewards entirely for free.
threats.investigation_rationale: Self-calls with `{value: X}` draw from the contract's own balance if the outer `msg.value` is insufficient or zero. This allows attackers to weaponize trapped user funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: External self-call `this.merkleClaim{value: mintFee}` inside a payable function without enforcing `msg.value == mintFee`

preconditions: PhiFactory contract has a trapped ETH balance > 0 (from user overpayments or direct transfers)
Attacker has a valid signature or Merkle proof to mint an NFT

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: `this.merkleClaim{ value: mintFee }` uses contract balance if `msg.value == 0`
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker initiates claim with 0 ETH, forcing the contract to pay its own mint fee
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker receives the minted NFT and any associated referral/verifier rewards without spending their own capital

economic_questions: Does the Solidity compiler allow `this.call{value: X}` to draw from contract balance when outer `msg.value` is 0?
Can the attacker route the mint fee to their own referral/verifier addresses to extract the ETH?
validation_requirements: Deploy PhiFactory, send 1 ETH to it (or simulate user overpayment)
Execute `claim` with `msg.value = 0` and verify if mint succeeds and ETH is distributed

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to the total trapped ETH balance in the factory

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Requires trapped ETH in the factory contract
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after a user accidentally overpays for a mint
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Direct theft of user funds that were intended to be refunded or withdrawn by admin

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users who overpaid, or the protocol admin (who would otherwise withdraw the excess)

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Solidity `this.func{value}` behavior regarding contract balance vs msg.value
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify `_processClaim` refund logic behavior when `etherValue_ == mintFee`

threats.title: Missing Signature Validation on Quantity Allows Arbitrary Minting
threats.hypothesis_description: The `encodeData_` payload signed by the protocol owner authorizes a user to mint an NFT, but it does not include the `quantity` parameter. The `quantity` is provided separately in the `MintArgs` struct. An attacker can take a valid signature intended for minting 1 NFT and change the `quantity` to any amount up to `maxSupply`, bypassing intended distribution limits.
threats.investigation_rationale: Cryptographic signatures must cover all critical parameters of an action. Omitting the quantity allows users to arbitrarily inflate their credential holdings.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `mintArgs_.quantity` is used for state updates and minting but is not part of the signed `encodeData_`

preconditions: Attacker has a valid signature or Merkle proof for at least 1 NFT

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: `_validateAndUpdateClaimState` checks `art.numberMinted + quantity_ > art.maxSupply` but does not verify `quantity_` against the signature
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker modifies `mintArgs_.quantity` in the transaction calldata
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker gains disproportionate number of credentials, which can be sold on secondary markets or used to farm referral/verifier rewards

economic_questions: Does the protocol intend for signatures to authorize unlimited mints per user?
Can the attacker extract more value in referral rewards than the cost of the mint fee?
validation_requirements: Submit transaction with `mintArgs.quantity = 100` and verify it succeeds

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Sufficient ETH to pay the mint fee for the inflated quantity
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on secondary market value of the NFT or internal reward routing

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Anytime a valid signature is obtained
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Dilutes the scarcity and credibility of the protocol's credentials

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: The credential creator and other legitimate holders
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Integrity of the entire credentialing system

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if `data_` in the signature is intended to encode quantity (it is currently treated as a generic bytes32)
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify `credMinted` boolean flag behavior (it does not prevent large initial quantities)

threats.title: Unbounded Curator Loop Allows Permanent Lock of Reward Distributions
threats.hypothesis_description: The `distribute` function in `CuratorRewardsDistributor` fetches all curator addresses for a given `credId` and loops over them to calculate and distribute rewards. An attacker can artificially inflate the number of curators by buying 1 share from thousands of different addresses. This causes the `distribute` function to exceed the block gas limit, permanently locking all accumulated rewards for that cred.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unbounded loops over user-controlled arrays are a classic DoS vector. The cost to add an address to the array is simply the cost of buying 1 share, which is minimal at low supplies.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `address[] memory distributeAddresses = credContract.getCuratorAddresses(credId, 0, 0);` followed by multiple `for` loops over the entire array

preconditions: A cred has accumulated a significant amount of rewards in the distributor

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: O(N) gas cost where N is the number of unique addresses holding shares
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker generates Sybil addresses and buys 1 share each to push N beyond the block gas limit (~30M gas on L2s)
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack; attacker might short the protocol or demand a ransom to sell the shares and reduce the array size (though selling also requires gas)

economic_questions: What is the exact gas cost per curator in the `distribute` loop?
How much capital is required to buy enough shares to hit the 30M gas limit?
validation_requirements: Calculate gas per iteration (includes external calls to `getShareNumber` and `depositBatch`)
Simulate the attack in Foundry to find the exact threshold of addresses needed to DoS

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of ~1000-2000 shares at the bottom of the bonding curve + gas for 2000 transactions
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.1 ETH total depending on L2 gas prices

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Before a large distribution is triggered
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Permanently locks curator rewards, destroying the incentive mechanism for that cred

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All legitimate curators of the targeted cred
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of all pending and future rewards
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total ETH balance for that `credId` in the distributor

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Gas cost of `depositBatch` which also loops over the addresses
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify if `stop_ = 0` in `getCuratorAddresses` strictly returns the full array

threats.title: Shared Merkle Root Storage Allows Denial of Service on Existing Art Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: In `PhiFactory`, the `credMerkleRoot` mapping is keyed by `(credChainId, credId)`. If a creator deploys multiple arts for the same `credId` (e.g., different tiers or updated versions), the `merkleRootHash` is overwritten globally for that `credId`. This immediately invalidates all pending Merkle claims for previously created arts associated with that `credId`, denying users their ability to mint.
threats.investigation_rationale: Global state variables shared across multiple independent assets (arts) create cross-contamination. A state update for Art B should not break the functionality of Art A.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `credMerkleRoot[credChainId][credId] = merkleRootHash;` overwrites the root based on credId, not artId

preconditions: An art exists for a specific `credId` using MERKLE verification
Users have valid proofs but have not yet claimed

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: `merkleClaim` reads `credMerkleRoot[art.credChainId][art.credId]` which may have been updated by a newer art
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Creator (or attacker if they can spoof creation) creates a new art for the same credId with a different Merkle root
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Creator can maliciously revoke access to an NFT after promising it, or accidentally break their own protocol

economic_questions: Is it intended for multiple arts to share the exact same Merkle root?
Can an attacker force the creation of a new art for a victim's credId?
validation_requirements: Create Art 1 with Root A. Verify claim works.
Create Art 2 for the same credId with Root B.
Attempt to claim Art 1 with Root A and observe the revert.

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of creating a new art (artCreateFee)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After a popular credential is announced but before users claim
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Breaks core protocol functionality for credential distribution

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users attempting to claim their earned credentials
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of credential access and wasted gas on failed claims
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Reputation and usability of the protocol

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if `createArt` allows anyone to create an art for an existing `credId` if they have a signature

threats.title: Targeted Lock Period Reset Allows Permanent Denial of Share Selling
threats.hypothesis_description: The `buyShareCredFor` function allows anyone to purchase shares on behalf of another user (`curator_`). Doing so updates `lastTradeTimestamp[credId_][curator_] = block.timestamp`, which resets the 10-minute lock period required before that user can sell. An attacker can continuously buy 1 share for a victim right before their lock period expires, permanently preventing them from selling their shares.
threats.investigation_rationale: State variables that restrict user actions (like lock periods) should only be updatable by the user themselves or through strictly controlled mechanisms, not arbitrarily by third parties.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `lastTradeTimestamp[credId_][curator_] = block.timestamp;` is set for the target address, not `msg.sender`

preconditions: Victim owns shares and intends to sell them
Attacker knows the victim's address and target credId

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Lock period is extended by `SHARE_LOCK_PERIOD` (10 minutes) every time a buy occurs for that address
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker can trap a whale's liquidity in the bonding curve, potentially allowing the attacker to sell their own shares at a higher price before the victim can exit

economic_questions: What is the cost to sustain this attack for 24 hours?
Can the attacker use `batchBuyShareCred` to lock multiple victims simultaneously?
validation_requirements: Simulate victim buying shares, wait 9 minutes, attacker calls `buyShareCredFor`
Verify victim's `sellShareCred` reverts after the original 10 minutes have passed

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect profit from preventing price crashes
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Variable depending on bonding curve position

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Requires low enough share price to make the attack economically viable
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When a large holder is expected to dump their shares
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Destroys liquidity guarantees of the bonding curve

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Inability to exit positions, leading to potential financial loss if price drops later

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Bonding curve price at high supplies to determine attack cost
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Check if `batchBuyShareCred` allows passing arbitrary `curator_` addresses

threats.title: Unauthenticated withdrawFor Allows Forced Extraction of User Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: The `withdrawFor(address from, uint256 amount)` function in `RewardControl` lacks access control, allowing anyone to trigger a withdrawal of rewards on behalf of any user. If `amount` is set to 0, it acts as a sentinel value to withdraw the user's entire balance. While the ETH is sent to the user's address (not stolen), this allows an attacker to forcibly disrupt the accounting of smart contracts that rely on accumulating balances, or grief contracts with expensive `receive()` functions.
threats.investigation_rationale: Financial withdrawal mechanisms should always require authorization from the account owner to prevent forced state transitions and accounting disruptions in composable DeFi environments.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `external` function with no `require(msg.sender == from)` or `onlyOwner` modifier

preconditions: Victim has a non-zero reward balance in `RewardControl`

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit; purely a griefing vector or a way to break composability for yield aggregators

economic_questions: Are there any integrated protocols that rely on the `balanceOf` mapping remaining stable?
Can this be used to force ETH into a contract to manipulate its internal balance checks?
validation_requirements: Call `withdrawFor` from an unrelated address and verify the victim's balance is transferred

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Low direct impact, but breaks composability assumptions

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify ETH is strictly sent to `from`

threats.title: Missing Return in Zero-Supply Fee Calculation Inflates View Prices
threats.hypothesis_description: In `BondingCurve._getCreatorFee`, there is a check `if (supply_ == 0) { creatorFee = 0; }`, but it lacks a `return` statement. Execution continues and overwrites `creatorFee` with a non-zero value based on the royalty rate. This causes the view functions `getBuyPriceAfterFee` and `getSellPriceAfterFee` to return inflated prices when the supply is zero, misleading users and frontends.
threats.investigation_rationale: Discrepancies between view functions (used by frontends for quoting) and actual execution logic (which correctly charges 0 fee at 0 supply) erode user trust and can cause users to overfund transactions.

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: `creatorFee` is overwritten by `(price_ * royaltyRate) / RATIO_BASE` immediately after being set to 0
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit; causes users to overpay in `msg.value` (excess is refunded) or deters them from buying due to artificially high displayed fees

economic_questions: Does the frontend rely exclusively on `getBuyPriceAfterFee` for transaction construction?
validation_requirements: Call `getBuyPriceAfterFee` at supply 0 and compare with the actual ETH deducted during `buyShareCred`

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When a cred is newly created or fully sold off
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Friction in user onboarding and initial share purchases

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Temporary lockup of excess ETH (refunded in the same tx)

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify refund logic in `buyShareCred` correctly returns the uncharged fee
